author: Robert Carr
description: A Nashville hot chicken sandwich with a pickle-buttermilk brined chicken thigh, a rough, crisp fried crust, spicy hot oil, bright pickle slaw, dill pickles, comeback sauce, and a toasted potato or brioche bun.

groceryIngredients.item: plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix

ingredients: 1 large boneless skinless chicken thigh, about 6 to 8 ounces
3 tablespoons pickle juice from a jar of dill pickle chips, divided
1 tablespoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, for the brine
3 cups neutral frying oil, such as peanut, canola, or vegetable oil, or enough for 2 inches in a small pot
1 sturdy potato bun or brioche bun
1/4 cup crinkle-cut hamburger dill pickle chips
3/4 cup plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix
1/2 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, for the comeback sauce
instructions: Trim any large loose pieces of fat from the chicken thigh.
If the chicken thigh is very thick, lightly pound it to an even 1/2 to 3/4 inch thickness.
In a bowl, whisk together 1/2 cup buttermilk, 2 tablespoons pickle juice from the dill pickle jar, 1 tablespoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, 1 large egg, and 1/2 teaspoon kosher salt.
Add the chicken thigh to the buttermilk brine and toss until fully coated.
Cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, or up to 24 hours.

Make the Nashville spice blend by mixing 1 tablespoon cayenne pepper, 1 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/2 teaspoon chili powder, 1/2 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/2 teaspoon onion powder, 1 1/2 teaspoons light brown sugar, 1/4 teaspoon mustard powder, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, 1/4 teaspoon kosher salt, and a pinch MSG if using.

Make the seasoned flour coating by whisking together 3/4 cup all-purpose flour, 2 tablespoons cornstarch, 1/4 teaspoon baking powder, 1 tablespoon cayenne pepper, 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/2 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/2 teaspoon onion powder, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/2 teaspoon kosher salt.
Drizzle 1 tablespoon of the buttermilk brine into the seasoned flour and rub it in with your fingers until small clumps form.

Make the comeback sauce by stirring together 1 tablespoon mayonnaise, 1 teaspoon ketchup, 1/4 teaspoon Dijon mustard, 1/4 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1/2 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, 1/2 teaspoon pickle juice from the dill pickle jar, a pinch garlic powder, and a pinch smoked paprika.
Refrigerate the comeback sauce until the sandwich is ready to assemble.
Set 1 wire rack inside a rimmed baking sheet for the breaded raw chicken, then set a second clean wire rack inside another rimmed baking sheet for the fried chicken.
Remove one piece of chicken from the brine and let the excess drip off.
Press the chicken firmly into the seasoned flour on both sides.
Dip the chicken briefly back into the brine.
Press the chicken into the seasoned flour a second time, packing the flour onto the surface to build a rough crust.
Gently shake off any loose dry flour so the coating looks rough but not dusty.
Place the breaded chicken on the raw-chicken wire rack.
Let the breaded chicken rest for 15 to 20 minutes while the oil heats.
Add 3 cups neutral frying oil to a small Dutch oven or heavy-bottomed pot, or use enough oil for 2 inches of depth, making sure the pot is not filled more than halfway.
Heat the oil to 350°F.

Make the slaw by mixing 3/4 cup plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix, 1 tablespoon mayonnaise, 1 1/2 teaspoons apple cider vinegar, 1/2 teaspoon pickle juice from the dill pickle jar, 1/2 teaspoon honey, a pinch celery seed, a pinch black pepper, and a small pinch of kosher salt.
Taste the slaw and adjust with more vinegar, pickle juice, honey, or salt until it tastes bright, tangy, and lightly creamy.
Spread 1/2 tablespoon softened unsalted butter over the cut sides of the potato bun or brioche bun.
Toast the bun in a dry pan until golden brown, then set aside.
Fry the chicken, keeping the oil between 325°F and 350°F, until deeply golden brown and the center reaches 175°F to 185°F, about 6 to 8 minutes depending on thickness.
Transfer the fried chicken to the clean wire rack so steam can escape.
Let the fried chicken rest for 3 to 5 minutes so the juices settle and the crust stays crisp.
Carefully ladle 2 tablespoons of the hot frying oil into the Nashville spice blend.
Whisk until the spices form a glossy red hot oil.
Brush the hot oil generously over both sides of the fried chicken just before assembling the sandwich.
Spread comeback sauce on the toasted bottom bun.
Add 1/4 cup crinkle-cut hamburger dill pickle chips to the bottom bun.
Add the Nashville hot chicken to the sandwich.
Top the sandwich with creamy slaw.
Spread comeback sauce on the toasted top bun.
Close the sandwich and serve immediately.
notes: Use boneless skinless chicken thighs because they stay juicy and are more forgiving than chicken breast.
For the pickle juice, buy a 16-ounce jar of crinkle-cut hamburger dill pickle chips or kosher dill pickle chips and use the liquid from that jar.
Avoid bread-and-butter pickles, sweet pickles, spicy-sweet pickles, and half-sour fermented pickles for this recipe. The pickle juice should taste salty, sour, dill-forward, and lightly garlicky.
Use a thin Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce. It should be vinegar-forward, salty, and peppery, not smoky, fruity, sweet, or extremely thick.
The brine is best after 8 to 12 hours, but 4 hours works and up to 24 hours is fine.
The pickle juice gives the chicken extra salt, acidity, and classic fried chicken sandwich flavor.
MSG is monosodium glutamate. It is a savory seasoning that makes the spice blend taste more savory.
For the juiciest texture, fry thighs past the safe minimum and aim for 175°F to 185°F in the center.
The small clumps in the seasoned flour help create a rougher, crunchier crust.
Resting the breaded chicken before frying helps the coating stick.
Use separate racks for raw breaded chicken and fried chicken. The clean rack also keeps the bottom crust from steaming soft.
The hot oil should be mixed with the spices after frying, not before, so the sugar and cayenne do not burn.
Use 1 tablespoon cayenne in the hot oil for a hot sandwich. Use 2 teaspoons for medium heat or 1 1/2 tablespoons for very hot.
The coleslaw mix should be a plain shredded cabbage and carrot mix, not a salad kit with dressing included.
The slaw should taste tangy and fresh on its own; that acidity is what keeps the sandwich from feeling heavy.
The comeback sauce, slaw, and pickles are important because they balance the heat and richness.
Serve the sandwich right after brushing the chicken with hot oil for the best crust.
Leftover chicken reheats best on a wire rack in a 375°F oven or air fryer until hot and crisp.
title: Nashville Hot Chicken Sandwich
totalTime: 50 minutes plus 4 to 24 hours brine time
